event: Early human settlement appears along the Lebanese coast, including Mesolithic and Neolithic communities near Byblos.
Byblos is settled, growing into one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world.
Canaanite (Phoenician) city-states such as Byblos, Sidon, and Tyre emerge as trading centers along the coast.
Byblos becomes a major exporter of cedar wood and papyrus to Egypt, deepening commercial ties.
The Phoenicians develop and spread an alphabetic writing system that becomes ancestral to Greek and Latin scripts.
Tyre rises to prominence as the leading Phoenician maritime power, founding colonies across the Mediterranean.
Phoenicians from Tyre traditionally found the colony of Carthage in North Africa.
The Assyrian Empire under Sennacherib asserts control over the Phoenician coastal cities.
Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II ends a long siege of Tyre, bringing the city under Babylonian dominance.
The Achaemenid Persian Empire absorbs Phoenicia, whose fleets become vital to Persian naval power.
Alexander the Great captures Tyre after a famous siege, opening the region to Hellenistic rule.
The Roman general Pompey annexes the region into the Roman province of Syria.
Roman Berytus (Beirut) flourishes as a colony and later hosts a renowned school of Roman law.
A devastating earthquake and tsunami destroy much of Berytus, including its famed law school.
Arab Muslim armies conquer the region following the Battle of Yarmuk, beginning the Islamic era.
Maronite Christians, fleeing persecution, consolidate communities in the rugged Mount Lebanon range.
Crusaders capture coastal cities including Tripoli, incorporating them into the Crusader states.
The Mamluks expel the last Crusaders from the coast, restoring Muslim control over Lebanon.
The Ottoman Empire under Selim I conquers the region, beginning four centuries of Ottoman rule.
Druze emir Fakhr al-Din II consolidates power in Mount Lebanon, laying foundations for Lebanese autonomy.
Fakhr al-Din II is executed by the Ottomans after expanding his semi-independent emirate.
Sectarian and political upheaval ends the long rule of the Shihab dynasty in Mount Lebanon.
Civil conflict between Druze and Maronites leads to massacres and European, especially French, intervention.
The Ottomans establish the autonomous Mount Lebanon Mutasarrifate under an international agreement.
The Syrian Protestant College, later the American University of Beirut, is founded.
A severe famine, worsened by Ottoman policies and an Allied blockade, kills a large share of Mount Lebanon's population.
France creates the State of Greater Lebanon under a League of Nations mandate after the Ottoman collapse.
The Lebanese Republic is proclaimed with the adoption of its first constitution.
Lebanon gains independence from France, and the National Pact establishes its confessional power-sharing system.
Lebanon becomes a founding member of the United Nations and the Arab League.
The last French troops withdraw, completing Lebanon's full sovereignty.
The Arab-Israeli war drives large numbers of Palestinian refugees into Lebanon.
A brief civil crisis prompts a U.S. military intervention to stabilize President Chamoun's government.
The Cairo Agreement regulates the armed Palestinian presence in Lebanon, fueling internal tensions.
The Lebanese Civil War erupts, beginning fifteen years of devastating sectarian conflict.
Syrian forces enter Lebanon, marking the start of a long military presence.
Israel invades Lebanon, besieging Beirut and forcing the PLO leadership to evacuate.
Suicide bombings of the U.S. embassy and multinational barracks in Beirut kill hundreds.
The Taif Agreement charts an end to the civil war and rebalances the confessional system.
The Lebanese Civil War formally ends after claiming roughly 150,000 lives.
Israel withdraws its forces from southern Lebanon after more than two decades of occupation.
The assassination of Prime Minister Rafik Hariri sparks the Cedar Revolution and the withdrawal of Syrian troops.
A 34-day war between Israel and Hezbollah devastates infrastructure across Lebanon.
The Syrian civil war drives over a million refugees into Lebanon, straining the country severely.
Mass protests erupt against corruption and economic collapse, toppling the government amid a deep financial crisis.
A massive explosion of stored ammonium nitrate at Beirut's port kills over 200 people and devastates the city.
An escalating Israel-Hezbollah conflict intensifies with Israeli strikes and a ground incursion before a ceasefire.
